coredata.dc:title: A solvent-engineered molecule fusion strategy for rational synthesis of carbon quantum dots with multicolor bandgap fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Zhan, Jing
Geng, Bijiang
Wu, Kuan
Xu, Gang
Wang, Liang
Guo, Ruiyun
Lei, Bo
Zheng, Fengfeng
Pan, Dengyu
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: We report a rational strategy for scalable production of highly fluorescent CQDs in high yields (50–92%) based on controlled molecule fusion of activated polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon in environmentally friendly polar solvents. By simply varying the composition of reaction solvents, their bandgaps were systematically manipulated, enabling the adjustment of fluorescent emissions from blue to red. Similar to conventional organic-soluble quantum dots but distinct from extensively reported water-soluble CQDs, the CQDs engineered in polar solvents are highly soluble in organic solvents and possess main features of intrinsic bandgap transitions, including overlapping of absorption and excitation spectra, excitation-independence fluorescence, the inversely proportional relationship of the bandgaps with fluorescence peak wavelength, and mono-exponential fluorescence decay. What's more, the red-fluorescent CQDs were encapsulated by liposomes, providing good biocompatibility and excellent in vitro and in vivo imaging capabilities.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Solvent-engineering of CQDs for fluorescence adjustment
Specific optical properties of TNP-fused CQDs
In vitro and in vivo imaging of r-GQDs capped by liposomes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11774216 , 21671129 ), the Shanghai Sailing Program (No. 16YF1404400 ), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 16ZR1412100 ), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Photographs of b-CQDs, g-CQDs, y-CQDs and r-CQDs dispersed in toluene, in daylight (left) and under UV irradiation (right). b) Their UV–vis absorption (ABS) spectra. c) Their normalized PL spectra. d) Their excitation (PLE) spectra.

a-d) PL spectra of (a) b-CQDs, (b) g-CQDs, (c) y-CQDs, and (d) r-CQDs dispersed in toluene under different excitation wavelengths. e) Their Eg values determined by the absorption edge as a function of the PL peak wavelength. f) Their time-resolved PL spectra.
TEM images and lateral size distribution diagrams of (a) b-CQDs, (b) g-CQDs, (c) y-CQDs and (d) r-CQDs.
XPS N 1s spectra of a) b-CQDs, b) g-CQDs, c) y-CQDs, and d) r-CQDs. Each band was deconvoluted following the literature.

a) Structure of lipo-r-CQD fluorescent probes using liposomes as a biocompatible stabilizer. b) UV/Vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of lipo-r-CQDs in water solution (inset: photograph of aqueous solution of lipo-r-CQDs under the UV light). c) Confocal fluorescence images of Hela cells using lipo-r-CQDs as the probe excited at 405, 488, 633 nm d) In vivo fluorescence images of Hela tumor-bearing nude mice in tumor sites at 0.5 and 1 h after intratumor injection of 200 μL lipo-r-CQDs. The color bars represent the fluorescence intensity. e) Cytotoxicity assessment of lipo-r-CQDs at higher doses for incubation time varied from 24 to 48 h using Hela cells.

A solvent-engineered MF strategy for high-yield production of multicolor fluorescent CQDs using TNP as active monomers, DMF or EtOH as the single or main solvent, and H2O or CH3COOH as the auxiliary solvent.
Used solvents, average lateral size, N/O content, PL peak position, energy-bandgap (Eg), QY and production yield of solvent-engineered CQDs.

head.ce:title: A solvent-engineered molecule fusion strategy for rational synthesis of carbon quantum dots with multicolor bandgap fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a rational strategy for scalable production of highly fluorescent CQDs in high yields (50–92%) based on controlled molecule fusion of activated polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon in environmentally friendly polar solvents. By simply varying the composition of reaction solvents, their bandgaps were systematically manipulated, enabling the adjustment of fluorescent emissions from blue to red. Similar to conventional organic-soluble quantum dots but distinct from extensively reported water-soluble CQDs, the CQDs engineered in polar solvents are highly soluble in organic solvents and possess main features of intrinsic bandgap transitions, including overlapping of absorption and excitation spectra, excitation-independence fluorescence, the inversely proportional relationship of the bandgaps with fluorescence peak wavelength, and mono-exponential fluorescence decay. What's more, the red-fluorescent CQDs were encapsulated by liposomes, providing good biocompatibility and excellent in vitro and in vivo imaging capabilities.

ce:section.ce:para: The Colloidal quantum dots offer a compelling combination of controllable solution growth and a wide range of spectral tunability for various applications such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs), solar cells, photocatalysis, bioimaging, and sensing [1–4]. Recently, carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted special attention owing to additional advantages such as enhanced photostability against photobleaching [5], low toxicity even at high concentrations (i.e. excellent biocompatibility and environmental friendliness) [6,7], low-cost production from abundant and cheap carbon sources [8,9], and facile surface functionalization for diverse applications [10–12]. Because of these attractive merits, colloidal CQDs are regarded as promising alternatives to conventional compound-based quantum dots containing toxic metal ions such as Cd2+ and Pb2+ [13,14].

To meet the real requirements for applications and enhance the fundamental understanding about the quantum confinement effect and edge effect of CQDs, it is highly desirable to develop a rational method for production of colloidal CQDs with variable dimensions, while their composition (C, N, O, or other elements) keep invariable. In order to successfully implement its applications in nearly all practical, preparing CQDs with fluorescent emission ranging over the entire visible spectrum is a key requirement [15,16]. To date, a number of CQDs with tunable emissions from blue to red, have been synthesized with various approaches [6,7,15–25,42]. For example, Hu et al. prepared multicolor fluorescent CQDs by controlling the dehydration reaction occurring during synthesis [15]; Liu et al. synthesized CQDs with a tunable wavelength from 464 to 556 nm by introducing glutaraldehyde into the precursor solution [17]; Bao et al. prepared photoluminescence-tunable CQDs with emission ranging from blue to orange by wet oxidation of carbon fibers [21]. However, many reported CQDs with full color emission were realized by selecting different excitation wavelengths for one kind of CQDs, tunning concentration and pH of CQDs solutions, without changing the chemical structure [16,18,26]. Such optical shifts cannot be truly labeled “tunable”, and these are often undesired in real world applications. What is truly tunable fluorescence is that all colors of CQDs can be distinguished not only by the fluorescent color, but also by the uniquely chemical and physical properties. The general reason of “pseudo tunable” fluorescence was that most of CQDs generally produced in complex mixtures, and difficult to separate with high efficiency. So the photoluminance (PL) emission wavelength of many CQDs reported in the literature is dependent on the excitation wavelength, which gives rise to fundamental limitations to the effective applications in the photoelectricity and energy fields [15,16,18,26]. Even though there are a few successful reports, most of them need separation and purification techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE), silica column chromatography, millipore centrifugal filter devices and dialyzing, leading to a low yield production (<40% in most cases) [5,17,19,25–29]. Therefore, these approaches are not suitable for industrial-scale production of high-quality CQDs.

In addition, most reported CQDs with full color emission are water-soluble [30–33]. There are no methods for the fabrication of organic-soluble CQDs with full color emission. The organic-soluble CQDs are also possessing good luminescence, and are attractive for applications in energy storage, and optoelectronic devices [34–36]. So far the most common synthetic approaches for organic-soluble CQDs are packaging with long aliphatic chains such as oleylamine (OAm) [34,35]. For example, by conjugated with oleylamine (OAm) Kwon et al. synthesized graphene quantum dots with organic solubility, and demonstrated that this quantum dots can employ as a dopant in organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) [34]. Very recently, we have reported a solvothermal synthesis of organic-soluble CQDs in nonpolar organic media (toluene) with long-wavelength emission [35]. Based on the molecular fusion (MF) mechanism proposed by our groups [5], it is highly possible to construct organic soluble CQDs with full color emission, from activated aromatic monomers and manipulate the MF mechanism to exactly control their size, composition, and functional groups.

Herein, we report a modified MF strategy for synthesizing organic-soluble CQDs with tunable lateral sizes and functional groups by varying the composition of reaction media (common polar solvents) under mild solvothermal conditions. Compared with previous report, we got multicolor CQDs by varying the composition of reaction solvents for the first time. Their bandgaps were solvent-engineered in a wide range (2.15–2.89 eV), allowing for the controlled adjustment of fluorescent emissions from 460 to 620 nm. The size and edge effects of organic-soluble CQDs in nonpolar solvents were tested to rule out the solvent effects from polar solvents, and their optical characteristics were revealed. Moreover, the solvent-engineered MF strategy is highly scalable owing to high production yields (50–92%) and facile purification by rotary evaporation. What's more, after encapsulated by liposomes, the red-emission CQDs also demonstrate excellent in vitro and in vivo imaging capabilities.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Pyrene was obtained from TCI. Glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH), nitric acid (HNO3), sulfuric acid (HSO4), dimethyl fumarate (DMF), ethanol (EtOH) and toluene were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent CO., Ltd. Deionized (DI) water (H2O) was produced using a Milli-Q system (R > 18.1 MΩ).

In a typical procedure for synthesis of CQDs, pyrene (2 g, TCI, purity > 98%) was nitrated into 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) in hot HNO3 (160 mL) at 80 °C under refluxing and stirring for 12 h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with deionized (DI) water (1 L) and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the acid. Then, CQDs were synthesized by a modified molecular fusion route from TNP in a one-pot solvothermal process [5,35]. Their optical properties were exactly tuned by a solvent engineering strategy, through which b-CQDs, g-CQDs, y-CQDs, and r-CQDs were produced in organic solvents of DMF/H2O (2:1 vol/vol), EtOH/CH3COOH (450:1 vol/vol), pure ethanol, and pure DMF, respectively. Typically, TNP powder (∼3.6 g) was synthesized by nitration of pyrene (4 g) in HNO3/H2SO4 mixed acid (3:1 vol/vol). TNP (0.1 g) was dispersed in appropriate organic solvents (45 mL) by ultrasonication (500 W, 40 kHz) for 30 min at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous suspension, which was transferred into a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (80 mL) and heated at 230 °C for 12 h in an oven. After cooled down to room temperature naturally, dark colored GQD suspensions were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove traces of insoluble impurities and dried in a rotary evaporation at 80–100 °C. Different PL colors of purified CQDs were finally obtained in a high yield (b-CQDs: ∼87%; g-CQDs: ∼50%; y-CQDs: 70%; r-CQDs: 90%). The solubility of b-CQDs, g-CQDs, y-CQDs, and r-CQDs in toluene is 1.01, 1.32, 1.51, and 1.64 mg mL−1 respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on FEI Tecnai F30 S-TWIN at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550 with a Cu Kα radiation. Raman-shift spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λex = 633 nm. FTIR spectra were recorded with a Bio-Rad FTIR spectrometer FTS165. XPS analysis was carried out on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. UV/Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Hitachi 3100 spectrophotometer and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with a Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a Horiba FM-4P time corrected single-photon-counting (TCSPC) system. PL quantum yileds of CQDs were determined by fluorescence lifetime and steady state spectroscopy (FLSP 920). All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

The afore-obtained red CQDs were precipitated by drying at 95 °C, and then re-dispersed in 5 ml chloroform for the concentration measurement. 18 mg of EYPC, 10 mg of DSPE-mPEG2000 and 10 mg of cholesterol were added into the flask with 5 mL of chloroform containing r-GQD, and then mixed uniformly at 35 °C. Then chloroform was volatilized by rotary evaporation under reduced pressure, and the resultant thin film adhering to the inside wall of flask was dried in argon atmosphere. Afterwards, 10 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4) was added into the flask, followed by shaking at 35 °C to dissolve the thin film in PBS solution. The final solution was extruded through a 0.22-μm polycarbonate membrane filter back and forth for five times, and the products of lipo-r-CQDs complexes were kept at room temperature for further use.

Hela cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle (DMEM) medium (Hyclone, USA) with 10% (vol/vol) fetal bovine serum (BI, USA). Approximately 2 × 105 Hela cells were seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and cultured using the same culture medium (2 mL per dish) at 37 °C under 5% CO2/95% air. All cells were incubated for at least 24 h until ∼80% confluence was reached. A lipo-r-CQDs aqueous solution was introduced into the cells with a final concentration of ∼10 μg/mL in the culture medium. After 0.5 h of incubation, the Hela cells were then washed three times with PBS to remove excessive lipo-r-CQDs. The cells were examined under a confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP5) using lasers of 405 nm, 488 nm and 633 nm. The optical absorption range of the mixture at 405, 488 and 633 nm excitation was measured at 420–480, 505–540 and 640–700 nm, respectively.

Female athymic nude mice with the age of 3–5 weeks were purchased from Shanghai silaike experimental animal Co., Ltd. and were housed in the SPF laboratory animal environmental. The Hela tumor models were established by subcutaneous injection of 1 × 107 cells in 150 μL serum-free DMEM medium into the right shoulder of nude mice. After ∼14 days, the mice bearing Hela tumors were used for fluorescence imaging when the tumor volume reached about 100 mm3.

In vivo fluorescence imaging was conducted using the Night OWL LB 983 in vivo Imaging System (Germany, Berthold). A nude mouse was subcutaneously injected with lipo-r-CQDs (20 μg/mL, 100 μl) at tumor site after being anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 1% pentobarbital. The autofluorescence was removed by the spectrum unmixing software. The peak excitation wavelength was 630 nm. The exposure time was 1.0 s for all fluorescence images.

The cell viability of lipo-r-CQDs was determined by the MTT assay with 3-(4,5-dimethyl-thiazole-2-yl)-2,5-phenyltetrazolium bromide of Hela cells. The cell viability was expressed as the percentage of viable cells in total cells. Hela cells were seeded in 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well). After pre-cultured for 24 h, the medium was replaced by a fresh one containing the lipo-r-CQDs at a dose of 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL for 24 and 48 h. In each well, 20 μl of MTT solution (5 mg ml−1) was added and then incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. 150 μl DMSO was substituted for the medium and the absorbance of each well at 490 nm was measured using a Microplate Reader (Thermo, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvent-engineering of CQDs for fluorescence adjustment
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 demonstrates a novel solvent-engineered strategy for the controlled synthesis of a series of fluorescent CQDs with different fluorescence colors under mild solvothermal conditions. The strategy is based on the rational MF mechanism, using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) molecules as the active monomers [35]. The solvent engineering followed two routes: one is using DMF and EtOH as a single solvent to fuse TNP molecules into narrow-bandgap CQDs, such as r-CQDs (red fluorescence) and y-CQDs (yellow fluorescence), respectively. The other is based on the mixture of main and auxiliary solvents to synthesize wide-bandgap (blue and green) emitting CQDs. For example, blue fluorescent CQDs (b-CQDs) were derived using a mixture of DMF/H2O (2:1 vol/vol), while green CQDs (g-CQDs) were from the TNP treatment in a mixture of EtOH/CH3COOH (450:1 vol/vol). By merely varying the components of TNP fusion media (solvents), a series of CQD colloids with the three RGB colors and other colors were facilely derived, since the properties of solvents can play a crucial role in tuning the size/composition and thus optical properties of the TNP-fused CQDs. It should be emphasized that the synthesis is not only environmentally friendly, without using any toxic agents, but also scalable to industrial production owing to high yields (92%, 69%, 50%, and 87% for r-CQDs, y-CQDs, g-CQDs, and b-CQDs, respectively). The high-yield production is ascribed to the effective and controllable fusion of TNP molecules into small-size and soluble carbon dots in polar solvents, while the formation of insoluble byproducts, such as non-fluorescent black carbon, is inhibited substantially and almost completely in the DMF solution. Moreover, during the whole preparation procedure, which involved a series of continuous steps, including the pre-synthesis, dispersion, and solvothermal fusion of TNP, separation and final drying of products, no inorganic salts or bases were added. Therefore, the purification of the products was simplified by convenient rotary evaporation to remove volatile solvents and small molecule impurities, without the need of time-consuming dialysis to remove vast inorganic species. In brief, the high-yield, environmentally friendly, and simplified synthetic strategy will promote the industrial-scale synthesis and practical applications of multifunctional CQDs within the frame of green chemistry-engineering.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific optical properties of TNP-fused CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the fundamental quantum size effects, the optical properties of the colloidal CQDs were systematically investigated in their solution states. All the dried CQDs can be stably dispersed in a variety of common polar and nonpolar organic solvents to form clear solutions. As shown in Fig. 1 a (left), clear solutions with dark yellow, green-yellow, bright yellow, and red colors were observed in the daylight for b-CQDs, g-CQDs, y-CQDs and r-CQDs, respectively, when the dried powders were dissolved in a nonpolar solvent (toluene). Under UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm), their toluene solutions emitted bright blue, green, yellow, and red lights, respectively, without using additional separation techniques to separate different fluorescent species from pristine mixtures (Fig. 1a, right). Recent studies have revealed that the strong polarity of polar solvents can substantially modify the optical properties of colloidal CQDs, leading to the remarkable redshift of PL peaks with increasing solvent polarity [37]. To avoid the interference of polar solvents to studies on the fundamental quantum size effects of the CQDs, we conducted all measurements of optical properties on their toluene solution except for the demonstration of the bioimaging applications.

Fig. 1b–d shows the typical absorption (ABS), PL, and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of their toluene solutions drawn in the same panels to reveal the direct spectral correlations for the same kind of the CQDs. We found that the main spectral characteristics and the inter-spectrum correlations for all the four kinds are extremely similar to those of conventional semiconductor quantum dots such as CdSe/ZnS core-shell quantum dots [38]. Firstly, all the ABS spectra are characterized by several strong absorption bands from the UV to visible regions without marked absorption tails [25]. For example, in the lower-energy region, the absorption spectrum of four CQDs exhibit distinct excitonic absorption bands at about 380, 460, 510, and 530 nm, indicating that these samples possessed different surface states (Fig. 1b). These absorption features are ascribed to the electronic transitions between a series of intrinsic quantum states from lower to higher energy. Secondly, the blue to red PL spectra consist of only one symmetrical and sharp peak, while for the toluene solutions of the two organic precursors, pyrene and TNP, several overlapping peaks constitute the broad, unsymmetrical PL spectra typical of organic fluorophores (Fig. S1). Moreover, there are small Stokes shifts between the lowest-energy absorption band and the PL maximum. For example, the PL peak of the r-CQDs is positioned at 620 nm, and their first low-energy absorption band is at 530 nm, giving a Stokes shift of ∼90 nm. Thirdly, the broad PLE spectra of the CQD series cover the whole UV–vis absorption bands and the PLE peaks near the absorption edge agree with the lowest-energy absorption bands. Fourthly, the PL peaks of the r-, y-, and g-CQDs do not shift at all when different excitation wavelengths were applied (Fig. 2 b–d). For the b-CQDs, the PL peak maximum shows extremely small shift within ∼10 nm when the excitation wavelength is varied from 340 to 440 nm (Fig. 2a). This excitation independence of PL is common for conventional quantum dots but unusual for widely reported CQDs [39,40]. What's more, all the CQDs show similar time-resolved PL spectra that are fitted with mono-exponential curves, giving a long lifetime of 4.90, 6.00, 6.33, and 6.42 ns for b-CQDs, g-CQDs, y-CQDs and r-CQDs, respectively (Fig. 2f). It is extensively reported that complex surface states coexist with excitonic states of sp [2] clusters in CQDs, leading to two competitive decay channels responsible for their multi-exponential PL decay feature.

Besides the specific and confined optical properties of the TNP-fused CQDs, the wide-range spectral tunability is directly demonstrated. The optical absorption edge in the visible region was found to red-shift from 380 nm for the b-CQDs to 530 nm for r-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1b. Similarly, the PL peak also red-shifts from 460 to 620 nm (Fig. 1c). These results indicate that the optical properties of the TNP-fused CQDs can be facilely engineered for various applications. As demonstrated in Fig. 5
, for example, the red-fluorescent CQDs can be used as ideal fluorescent probes for in vitro and in vivo imaging. The optical bandgaps (E
g ) can be determined from the equation: E
g (eV) = 1240/λ edge, where λ edge is the onset of the first excitonic absorption band in the direction of long wavelengths [25]. The calculated E
g value ranges from 2.14 to 2.82 eV, as listed in Table 1 . As expected, an inversely proportional relationship between E
g and the PL wavelength is well established (Fig. 2e). Table 1 also lists the absolute PL quantum yields (QYs) of the CQD series determined by the integral ball method. It is noted that the narrow-bandgap CQDs have higher QYs (59.00% for the y-CQDs; 27.38% for r-CQDs) under the optimal excitation wavelength (i.e., 510 nm for y-CQDs; 550 nm for r-CQDs) using an absolute quantum efficiency measurement system (Table 1). The QYs determined by a relative method are closed to the absolute one (27.5% for b-CQDs, 26.7% for g-CQDs, 71.7% for y-CQDs and 40.0% for r-CQDs, Figs. S2–S5).

In order to obtain further insight into the specific optical properties of these CQDs, their structure, size, composition, and functional groups were characterized in detail by diverse techniques. Their structures were first examined by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). The XRD patterns of all dried samples are characterized by a broad diffraction peak indexed to a graphite-like structure with a larger (002) layer spacing of 3.4 Å (Fig. S6). No diffraction peaks from inorganic impurities or unreacted TNP were observed, indicating that the products after rotary evaporation are pure albeit no further purification by dialysis. Among the four samples, the y-CQDs have a highest degree of graphitization, as confirmed by the sharper (002) diffraction peak. Raman spectra also show similar results (Fig. S7). Two characteristic G and D bands of graphitized CQDs were centered at 1580 and 1370 cm−1, respectively. Albeit the identical Raman shifts for these samples, the G to D intensity ratio of the y-CQDs is significantly larger than that of the other samples. The stronger G band than the D band also indicates that the y-CQDs have high graphitization and few defects responsible for their high QY. In the high-resolution TEM images of typical CQDs, (100) lattice fringes of the graphitized structure with a spacing of 0.21 nm were well resolved (Insets in Fig. 3c).

The CQD series synthesized under the different solvent conditions were further examined by low-magnification TEM. A nearly monodisperse size distribution was observed in their TEM images and their average lateral sizes were calculated according to the size distribution (Fig. 3). We found that the solvents used in the synthesis can regulate the lateral sizes of the TNP-fused CQDs. In DMF alone, the largest CQDs (r-CQDs) were yielded with an average lateral size (3.98 nm) (Fig. 3d). With the addition of H2O into DMF, however, the average lateral size is substantially reduced from 3.98 to 2.25 nm. For the synthesis in EtOH alone, resulting yellow-emitting CQDs have an average lateral size of 2.93 nm (Fig. 3c). Similarly, the addition of CH3COOH as an auxiliary solvent into EtOH also reduces the average size (Fig. 3b). The solvent-regulating size effect is ascribed to different solvating capabilities of the used solvents dispersing the organic-soluble TNP precursor in them. DMF has a stronger ability to solvate organic-soluble TNP than EtOH owing to the presence of two ester groups (-COO-). Indeed, brown-yellow TNP powder can be immediately dissolved in DMF to form a black TNP/DMF complex solution via strong hydrogen bonds between the two ester groups and the nitro-groups of TNP (Scheme 1). In contrast, TNP can only be dispersed in EtOH to form a turbid brown dispersion under strong ultrasonication. The soluble TNP/DMF complex as an effective intermediate facilitates the larger-scale fusion of TNP molecules under the solvothermal conditions, leading to the increased “growth” of the r-CQDs with the largest sizes. Moreover, the soluble TNP/DMF complex can prevent the formation of insoluble by-products and thus increase the yield of soluble CQDs (92%). The addition of the auxiliary solvents (H2O and CH3COOH) into the main solvents (DMF and EtOH) can reduce the dispersibility of TNPs in the mixed solvents, leading to lower-scale fusion of TNP molecules into smaller CQDs. Exact mechanisms by which solvents control the fusion scale of TNP are to be further studied, but the observed solvent effects provide new insight into the rational and controlled synthetic design for large-sized colloidal CQDs with fluorescence emission even expanded into near IR.

The solution quasi-growth of the TNP-fused CQDs with tunable composition and functional groups were also examined by X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS surveys of these CQDs presented in Fig. S8 show the C 1s peak (285 eV) of the sp2 carbon core and the peaks of N 1s (400 eV), and O 1s (531 eV) at the edge or shell. Thus, the C, N, and O percentages of the four samples are measured by XPS and listed in Table 1. We found that the use of DMF in the synthesis of b- and r-CQDs produced a lower O content of ∼12 at.%, about half that of the g- and y-CQDs synthesized in EtOH. The addition of the auxiliary solvents induced small change of the C, N, and O composition, suggesting that the composition adjustment is controlled by the main solvents. The high O content (∼24 at.%) of the g- and y-CQDs is largely ascribed to the functionalization by -NO2 groups at higher concentrations or attributed to solvent effect [29]. As confirmed by the N 1s spectra presented in Fig. 4, strong -NO2 signals at 405.8 eV were observed in the g-CQDs and y-CQDs (Fig. 4b, c), while the NO2 signal is absent in the b-CQDs and very weak in the r-CQDs (Fig. 4a, d) [5]. Besides -NO2 in the N 1s spectra, pyridine N was also detected at ∼399 eV in the all samples [11,19,26]. The detection of pyridine N and -NO2 reveals that the TNP-fused CQDs are nitrogen doped as a form of pyridine N and functionalized by -NO2 at the edge. The presence of -NO2 groups in the TNP-fused CQDs also supports the MF quasi-growth mechanism using the NO2-substituted pyrene (TNP) as the monomers. In the high-resolution C 1 s spectra, the signals of C=C at ∼284.5 eV and C-N at ∼286.0 eV were observed (Fig. S9). The high-resolution O 1s spectra of the CQD series can be deconvoluted into two peaks at ∼532.9 and 531.5 eV, ascribed to NO2 and C-O groups, respectively (Fig. S9).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the TNP-fused CQDs were also used to characterize their chemical structures. Interestingly, the FT-IR spectra of most TNP-fused CQDs (r-, y-, and g-CQDs) show similar spectroscopic characteristics compared with that of the TNP precursor (Fig. S10). For TNP, two main IR vibration bands at 1180 and 1524 cm−1 are ascribed to -NO2 substitutes and C=C bonds of benzene rings, respectively. The two typical IR vibration bands of TNP were also observed in the r-CQDs, y-CQDs, and g-CQDs. The similar FT-IR spectra among TNP and TNP-fused CQDs provide direct evidence for the molecule fusion mechanism of the TNP-fused CQDs. For sample b-CQDs, however, the FT-IR spectrum is distinct from those of TNP and other CQDs. The -NO2 vibration band of the b-CQDs disappears, which is consistent with the XPS N 1s analysis.

Based on the above detailed characterization of the CQD samples in terms of size, composition and functional groups, the close correlations of their unusual optical properties with their microstructures can be revealed. First of all, the quantum size effects characteristic of quantum dots should dictate the main optical features of the bandgap fluorescent CQDs. As shown in Table 1, the average lateral size of the CQD series steadily increases from 2.25 to 3.98 nm. With increasing the size, the PL peak redshifts and the corresponding bandgap decreases consistently. It should be pointed out that apart from the size effects, composition variation within quantum dots may also induce the change in their bandgaps. Recent studies on CQDs have suggested that the incorporated O and N heteroatoms in CQDs could induce opposite effects on the change in bandgaps: a high concentration of O species bonded with sp2 clusters may reduce the bandgap by inducing rich surface states, while the N doping may increase the bandgap. The opposite composition effects and uncontrollable composition changes in the synthesis of CQDs may lead to the misunderstanding of the real correlations of the optical properties of CQDs with their microstructures. To clarify the complex and confusing structure-property relationship of CQDs, we individually discuss the size and composition effects on the bandgap for the TNP-fused CQD series with other variables are unchanged. According to the composition, size and bandgap data from Table 1, the four samples fall into three groups (b, r), (g, y), and (b, g). For the first group (b, r), which has a nearly identical (C, N, O) composition with a low O content, there is a large difference in lateral size between the b- and r-CQDs (3.98 nm - 2.25 nm = 1.73 nm), which leads to a large difference in bandgap (2.82 eV - 2.14 eV = 0.68 eV) owing to the quantum size effect. Similarly, for the second group (g, y) with the nearly same composition and a high O content, the small size difference (0.44 nm) between the two components determines the small bandgap difference (0.31 eV). For the third group, there is an insignificant size difference (∼0.24 nm) between the b- and g-CQDs, but the bandgap difference (∼0.23 eV) is not ignorable. In this case, the effect of functional groups, particularly -NO2 groups, on the bandgap change becomes important. The substitution of NO2 groups in pyrene as electron-acceptors can induce larger energy difference between the HOMO and LUMO orbitals, as confirmed by a large redshift in the PL emission from 395 nm in pyrene to 510 nm in TNP (Fig. S1). A similar redshift effect induced by the NO2 groups should exist between the b-CQDs (no NO2 groups) and g-CQDs (a high concentration of NO2 groups). Therefore, in our case, we believe band-gap transitions and surface states to be the synergistic factor controlling the PL variations [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo imaging of r-GQDs capped by liposomes
ce:section.ce:para: The superior optical properties of the colloidal multicolor CQDs as well as their ability to dissolve in various solvents enable their applications in various areas such as solution-processable photoelectronic devices. The r-CQDs could be used as desirable fluorescent probes for in vitro and in vivo imaging owing to a combination of merits, such as a high QY, a long-wavelength emission (620 nm), and a broad excitation spectrum. However, these r-CQDs can't be directly used for bioimaging because of their insolubility in water. Liposomes are effective, biocompatible carriers to encapsulate lipid-soluble drug molecules or fluorescent nanoparticles, and therefore are extensively employed in nano-medicine for drug loading and in vivo imaging [43,44]. Because the lipid-soluble r-CQDs can be inserted into the lipid bilayers of liposomes, as shown in Fig. 5a, we explored their encapsulation by amphiphilic liposomes to form liposome-r-CQD nanostructures (lipo-r-CQDs), which combine the superior optical properties of r-CQDs and the biocompatiblity of the liposomes for bioimaging applications (Fig. S11). The lipo-r-CQDs were facilely fabricated through a thin lipid film hydration method. A thin film of lipids and r-CQDs was formed in a round-bottom flask by evaporating organic solvents, followed by a freeze-drying procedure to ensure the complete removal of the remaining solvents. After re-hydration with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution, the resulting hybrid nanoparticles became soluble in PBS solution. DLS was used to determine the average size of lipo-r-CQDs nanostructures. As shown in Fig. S13, the aqueous dispersion of liposome-CQDs exhibited an average diameter of 158 nm. What's more, the size of the lipo-r-CQDs was further confirmed by TEM. Fig. S14 reveals that their sizes are distributed between 50 and 150 nm. Such discrepancies between the hydrodynamic diameter determined by DLS and the average size determined by TEM have also been previously reported by others and are due to the measured hydrodynamic diameter in the case of DLS. The changed PL spectra after surface functionalization with liposome is probable ascribed to the increased size (∼4 nm) of CQDs (Fig. S14). Fig. S14 show the HRTEM imaging of r-CQDs with a spacing of 0.21 nm. The QYs of lipo-r-CQDs in aqueous solution are 13.3% (Fig. S15). It can be seen that the QYs of r-CQDs have been decreased after encapsulated by liposome. The lower QYs of lipo-r-CQDs are ascribed to the partially aggregation of r-CQDs.

The lipo-r-CQD nanostructures have four distinct advantages. Firstly, with the encapsulation of the liposomes, the hydrophobicity of the r-CQDs was converted into hydrophilicity to make them biocompatible for in vitro and in vivo imaging (Fig. S11). Secondly, a lipid bilayer can prevent the carried r-CQDs from freely diffusing out of the nanostructure, thereby enhancing the encapsulation of r-CQDs. Thirdly, the liposome encapsulation does not substantially modify the PL spectra of the r-CQDs or markedly lower the emission brightness (Fig. 5b). Similar to the r-CQDs, the PL spectra of b-CQDs, g-CQDs and y-CQDs also shifts to 540, 545 and 605 nm after encapsulated by liposome (Fig. S12). Although the PLE spectra of the r-CQDs are modified after the encapsulation, the red fluorescence of the hybrid structure can be excited in a wide wavelength range. Moreover, the hybrid structure shows high photostability against photobleaching. After their water solution was irradiated for 2 h by strong UV light, the PL intensity shows a small attenuation of 10% (Fig. S16). Finally, taking into account the future targeted imaging purposes, the surface of the lipo-r-CQDs can be further functionalized with targeting molecules. To our best knowledge, the novel hybrid nanostructure using CQDs as superior probes has not been explored to date for potential biomedical applications.

To demonstrate the in vitro imaging application of the lipo-r-CQDs, Hela cells as a model were incubated with a concentration of lipo-r-CQDs (25 mg L−1) in Dulbecco modified eagle medium (DMEM) for 0.5 h at 37 °C. Bright field images by a laser scanning confocal microscope show that Hela cells were well cultured. Confocal microscopic images were excited with lasers with excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 633 nm, and detected in the range of 440–480, 500–530, and 640–700 nm, respectively. As shown in Figure 5c, the lipo-r-CQDs were internalized into the cytoplasm of the cells and emitted bright fluorescence that can be excited by a wide wavelength range from 405 to 633 nm. Importantly, the self-fluorescence of cells can be greatly reduced by using 633 nm.

Based on the in vitro results, we then examined the lipo-r-CQDs for fluorescence imaging in vivo. Initially, the Hela tumor-bearing nude mice were selected as the animal model. When the tumors reached approximately 200 mm [3], the mice were treated with intratumor injection of lipo-r-CQDs. Figure 5d shows that, after injection of 0.5 h, under the excitation wavelength of 630 nm, strong red fluorescence can be clearly observed in the tumor area where lipo-r-CQDs are injected. After injection of 1 h, the lipo-r-CQDs also accumulate in the tumor area and significant fluorescence contrast was observed in comparison with other tissues. The in vivo observation and findings from the animal experiments demonstrate that the r-CQDs coated by liposomes retain PL brightness in the in vivo test. These results demonstrate that the lipo-r-CQDs can potentially use as an excellent labeling material for in vivo imaging.

The cytotoxicity of the lipo-r-CQDs was evaluated using the MTT assay. Fig. 5e shows the evolution of cell viability within 24 and 48 h at doses of 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 mg L−1. At the imaging dose of 25 mg L−1, the cell viability keeps over 85% after 24 h incubation, revealing low cytotoxicity at low doses. This preliminary result indicates that the fluorescent lipo-r-CQDs can serve as a low-cytotoxicity biological imaging agent.

In conclusion, we have synthesized of multi-color fluorescent CQDs with controlled size, composition, and functional groups, by simply varying the composition of reaction solvents. In this strategy, DMF and EtOH were used as a main solvent to increase the size of TNP-fused CQDs and thus decrease their optical bandgap for long-wavelength yellow-red fluorescence emissions. Water and alcohol were as an auxiliary solvent to decrease their lateral size for blue-green emissions. Resulting CQDs offer superior optical properties with a QY up to 59% for yellow fluorescence. Importantly, the CQDs possess main features of true quantum dots with surface well passivated by N-containing species such as pyridine and oxidized N, including excitation-independence fluorescence, the inversely proportional relationship of the bandgaps with fluorescence peak wavelength, overlapping of absorption and excitation spectra, and mono-exponential fluorescence decay. All these unique optical properties were found to be controlled by the quantum size effect and slightly modified by surface groups. Besides the successful demonstration of their bioimaging applications in vitro and in vivo, the CQDs can find a large array of novel applications in light emitting diodes, solar cells, hydrogen production, fuel cells and supercapacitors. Our findings will highlight the importance of the scalable solvothermal growth for solution-processable graphene materials and exert a great impact on fluorescence microscopy for clinical diagnostic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11774216, 21671129), the Shanghai Sailing Program (No. 16YF1404400), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 16ZR1412100), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No.IRT13078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online Data Online Data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.12.075.
